questions.question: According to Ibn Battuta, a Moroccan traveller to India during the Delhi Sultanate, the method of postal system in which the royal horses were used to transfer packages, is called:
questions.solution: The Moroccan traveler Ibn Battuta noted that the Delhi Sultanate had a highly efficient postal system. The horse-post, called Uluq, used royal horses stationed at distances of every four miles to carry packages and news. The foot-post was called Dawa.

questions.question: During the rule of which of the following Mughal rulers did Sunnis and Shias meet in one mosque and Christians and Jews in one church to pray, thus following the principle of 'universal peace' (sulh-i kul)?
questions.solution: Akbar was the Mughal ruler who promoted the philosophy of Sulh-i-kul (Universal Peace). He built the Ibadat Khana (House of Worship) where scholars of all religions—Sunnis, Shias, Christians, Jews, and Hindus—could debate. This policy emphasized religious tolerance and a common ethical code over dogmatic differences.

questions.question: The Third Battle of Panipat in 1761 was fought between Ahmad Shah Abdali and which Indian power?
questions.solution: The Third Battle of Panipat (1761) was a major conflict between the Maratha Empire, led by Sadashivrao Bhau, and the invading Afghan forces of Ahmad Shah Abdali. The Marathas suffered a heavy defeat, which halted their expansion toward North India and paved the way for British rise.

questions.question: The Aguada Fort, which is a monument to Portuguese construction and engineering, is situated in which of the following states of India?
questions.solution: Aguada Fort is a 17th-century Portuguese fort standing on Sinquerim Beach in Goa. It was constructed in 1612 to guard against the Dutch and Marathas and served as a crucial watering station for ships passing through the Arabian Sea.

questions.question: From the 11ᵗʰ through the 18ᵗʰ centuries, what type of literature was the most prominent in medieval India?
questions.solution: Between the 11th and 18th centuries, devotional poetry associated with the Bhakti and Sufi movements was the most prominent form of literature. Saints like Kabir, Meerabai, and Tulsidas composed verses in vernacular languages, making spiritual teachings accessible to the common masses.

questions.question: Which of the following was the military department during the reign of Alauddin Khilji?
questions.solution: The Diwan-i-Arz was the military department in the Delhi Sultanate. Under Alauddin Khilji, this department became extremely important as he introduced the Dag (branding of horses) and Huliya (descriptive roll of soldiers) systems to maintain a powerful standing army.

questions.question: Who among the following was the renowned poet-saint of the Bhakti movement known for composing dohas (couplets)?
questions.solution: Kabir was a 15th-century mystic poet and saint whose writings influenced the Bhakti movement. He is most famous for his dohas (two-line couplets) which used simple language to challenge religious orthodoxy and emphasize the unity of God.

questions.question: Which of the following administrative features was common to both Pallava and Pandya kingdoms?
questions.option_1: Village assemblies were called Sabha and Ur
questions.option_3: Division of the kingdom into Mandalams and Nadus
questions.solution: A common feature of South Indian administration in both Pallava and Pandya kingdoms was the existence of village assemblies. The Sabha was usually an assembly of Brahmin landholders, while the Ur was a more general village assembly found in non-Brahmadeya villages.

questions.question: The First Battle of Panipat was fought in 1526, replacing the Lodi Dynasty with Mughal rule in India. It was fought between Ibrahim Lodi and _____.
questions.solution: The First Battle of Panipat (1526) was fought between Babur and the Sultan of Delhi, Ibrahim Lodi. Babur's use of field artillery and superior tactics led to a decisive victory, marking the end of the Delhi Sultanate and the birth of the Mughal Empire.

questions.question: The Red Fort in Delhi was constructed during the reign of which of the following Mughal emperors?
questions.solution: The Red Fort (Lal Qila) in Delhi was commissioned by Emperor Shah Jahan in 1638 when he decided to shift his capital from Agra to the newly planned city of Shahjahanabad. It is an masterpiece of Mughal architecture made of red sandstone.

questions.question: During the Mughal period, the Ain-i-Dahsala system was introduced by _____.
questions.solution: The Ain-i-Dahsala (or Zabti system) was a land revenue system introduced by Akbar with the help of his finance minister, Raja Todar Mal. It calculated revenue based on the average produce and prices of the last ten years, providing stability to both the state and the farmers.

questions.question: Gyaneshwar (1275 A.D.) holds a unique position in Marathi literature, primarily because he was:
questions.option_2: the first and foremost Bhakti poet
questions.solution: Gyaneshwar (or Jnanadeva) was a 13th-century saint and philosopher. He is considered the first and foremost Bhakti poet of Maharashtra. His commentary on the Bhagavad Gita, known as the Jnanesvari, laid the foundation for the Varkari tradition and Marathi literature.

questions.question: Which department of the Delhi Sultanate was responsible for military organisation and administration of the empire’s army?
questions.solution: The Diwan-i-Arz was the department responsible for the military during the Delhi Sultanate. It was headed by the Ariz-i-Mamalik, whose duties included recruiting soldiers, inspecting the cavalry, and managing the logistics of the army.

questions.question: Which department, headed by Dabir-i-Khas, handled royal correspondence and official communication during the Delhi Sultanate?
questions.solution: The Diwan-i-Insha was the department of royal correspondence. Headed by the Dabir-i-Khas, it dealt with the drafting of firmans (royal orders) and all official communications between the Sultan and his provincial governors or foreign rulers.

questions.question: Who among the following is the founder of the Pala dynasty?
questions.solution: Gopala was the founder of the Pala Dynasty in the 8th century (around 750 CE). He was elected as the ruler by the local chieftains of Bengal to end the period of anarchy (Matsyanyaya) that followed the death of Shashanka.

questions.question: Market control under Alauddin Khilji aimed mainly to ________.
questions.option_4: check price inflation for army provisions
questions.solution: Alauddin Khilji implemented strict market control and price regulation primarily to check price inflation. This allowed him to maintain a large, well-equipped standing army with low salaries, as the cost of essential provisions and army supplies was kept artificially low.

questions.question: At the time of colonial period, the Asaf Jahi dynasty was established in _______.
questions.solution: The Asaf Jahi dynasty was founded by Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I in 1724. He established an independent state in Hyderabad as the Mughal Empire began to weaken. The dynasty ruled the Hyderabad State until it joined the Indian Union in 1948.

questions.question: The governor of a province (bhukti) in the Pala and Pratihara empire was called:
questions.solution: In the Pala and Pratihara administrative systems, the empire was divided into bhuktis (provinces). The governor or head of a bhukti was titled the Uparika. The provinces were further divided into visayas (districts) headed by Visayapatis.

questions.question: Who among the following compiled the Mrga-pasi-sastra in the 13ᵗʰ century, which provided an account of beasts and birds of hunting?
questions.solution: Hamsadeva was a 13th-century scholar who compiled the Mrga-pasi-sastra. This unique text is a specialized treatise on zoology and hunting, providing detailed descriptions of various animals and birds used in or targeted during hunting expeditions.

questions.question: In which of the following volumes of Akbar Nama, Akbar’s administration, household, army, the revenues and the geography of his empire is discussed by Abul Fazl?
questions.solution: The Akbar Nama, written by Abul Fazl, consists of three volumes. The third volume is known as the Ain-i-Akbari. It is a detailed administrative manual covering the Mughal administration, army, revenues, household, and the diverse geography and culture of India.

questions.question: Under Akbar, who was responsible for revenue and financial supervision across the entire Mughal Empire?
questions.solution: Under Akbar's administrative reforms, the Diwan-i-Kul (also known as the Wazir) was the head of the revenue and finance department. He supervised all financial transactions and land revenue collections across the empire.

questions.question: Which Rajput ruler founded the Gurjara-Pratihara dynasty in Malwa during the 8ᵗʰ century CE?
questions.solution: The Gurjara-Pratihara dynasty in the Malwa region was founded by Nagabhatta I in the 8th century. He is famous for successfully defending Western India against Arab invasions, specifically the campaigns of Junaid and Tamin.

questions.question: The Tripartite Struggle was a prolonged conflict among the Gurjara-Pratiharas, Palas, and which southern Indian dynasty?
questions.solution: The Tripartite Struggle was a long-running conflict over the control of Kannauj (the symbol of imperial power in North India). It involved three major powers: the Palas of Bengal, the Gurjara-Pratiharas of Malwa/Rajasthan, and the Rashtrakutas of the Deccan.

questions.question: Who was the first Portuguese explorer to reach India in 1498, arriving at Calicut on the Malabar Coast?
questions.solution: Vasco da Gama was the first European to reach India by sea. Following the Cape Route around Africa, he landed at Calicut (Kozhikode) on May 20, 1498, where he was received by the local ruler, the Zamorin. This event opened the doors for direct maritime trade between Europe and Asia.

questions.question: Which Delhi Sultan captured Bukka and Harihara after the fall of the Kakatiya Kingdom?
questions.solution: Following the fall of the Kakatiya kingdom of Warangal, Muhammad bin Tughlaq captured the brothers Harihara and Bukka and took them to Delhi, where they were converted to Islam. They were later sent back to the Deccan to suppress rebellions, where they eventually reverted to Hinduism and founded the Vijayanagara Empire.
